title: Chapter 32: The Morning
content: Paris did not wake. Paris was already awake — had been awake for hours, for days, for the entire interminable week of Fashion Week at its scorching, breathless peak. The city hummed at a frequency just below hearing, a current in the air that made the hairs on Jane's arms stand at attention before she even opened her eyes.

She woke before Edward. That was the first small victory of the day, and she clung to it — the fact of being conscious before him, watching the slow rise and fall of his chest in the gray pre-dawn, while her mind ran through the checklist she'd already memorized a hundred times. His face in sleep was almost gentle. The hard line of his jaw softened. The mouth that could cut like a blade was slack, nearly boyish.
She slipped from the sheets without disturbing them.

The bathroom was marble and brass, a shrine to money Jane had once found impressive and now found merely cold. She turned the shower hot enough to sting and stood under it, letting the steam fill her lungs, letting the water beat against her shoulder blades like a warning. She thought: *Tonight*. She thought: *There is no version of tonight where I walk out of this unchanged*. She thought: *Stop thinking. Wash your hair*.

She dressed carefully — not the gown. The gown was for tonight, hanging in its garment bag in the closet like a coiled serpent, oatmeal silk hiding crimson beneath. She chose instead a simple black turtleneck, dark trousers, flat shoes. Anonymous. Forgettable. The kind of clothing that made you a backdrop, not a focal point. She had learned that trick years ago, in the atelier, when she'd wanted to be noticed for her work and not her body. Today she didn't want to be noticed at all.
She took her phone into the bathroom and locked the door.
Four messages. She read them in order.
Richard: *Legal docs filed and timestamped. Ready to go on your signal.*

She exhaled. Richard Mason, who had every reason to despise Edward Rochester and had instead chosen the slower, more surgical violence of law. The documents were lodged in three jurisdictions now — French, British, American. Whatever happened tonight, the paper trail was permanent. Edward could not un-file what had been filed.
Helen: *Story locked. Editor doesn't know. Publishing at 8:47 PM Paris time.*

Helen Burns, who had taught Jane everything she knew about moral clarity, had written the story of her career. Forty-seven hundred words. Every claim sourced. Every allegation documented. The kind of journalism that ended careers and won prizes, usually in that order. The editor didn't know — couldn't know, because Edward had people everywhere, and one whispered word would kill the piece before it breathed. At 8:47 PM Paris time, Helen would press the button herself.
Bertha: *Server back door tested. 47 seconds from trigger to full deployment. Don't be late.*

Forty-seven seconds. Jane had watched Bertha test it twice. The first time, Jane had held her breath. The second time, she'd counted. Forty-seven seconds from the moment Bertha's fingers hit the keys to the moment every screen in the venue would switch from the Thornfield feed to the evidence. Financial fraud. Exploitation. The photographs. The contracts. The women whose names had been erased from their own work. All of it, projected larger than life, inescapable and irrefutable. Simple. Clean. Devastating.
And then the fourth message.
Adèle: *I'm scared.*

Jane stared at it for a long time. Adèle Varens, nineteen years old, who had been Edward's ward since she was a child, who had watched her guardian build an empire on skeletons and said nothing — not out of loyalty, Jane knew, but out of the particular paralysis of the young when confronted with the monstrous. Adèle had come to Jane three months ago with trembling hands and a flash drive full of files she'd copied from Edward's private server. She had been scared then, too.
Jane typed: *So am I. But we're doing it scared. That's what brave means.*

She sent it before she could second-guess the words, then turned the phone face-down on the marble and stared at herself in the mirror. Her own eyes stared back — gray, steady, the eyes of a woman who had spent her entire life being underestimated and had learned to weaponize it.

Breakfast was served in the suite's small dining alcove, sunlight pouring through floor-to-ceiling windows onto white linen and silver service. The coffee was perfect. Edward had made it himself — he always did, when he was in a good mood, which was to say when everything was going exactly according to his plan. He measured the grounds, timed the bloom, poured at precisely two hundred degrees. He knew Jane took hers with one sugar and no cream, and he set the cup before her with the particular tenderness of a man who believed that attention to detail was indistinguishable from love.
"Croissant?" He offered the basket, and his fingers brushed hers as she took one. Still warm. Butter-rich. It dissolved on her tongue like a lie.

Edward was luminous this morning. That was the only word for it. He wore a charcoal shirt, sleeves rolled to the forearms, and the morning light caught the silver at his temples and the fine architecture of his jaw. He looked like a man on the eve of coronation — which, in a sense, he was. Tonight's show would be the crowning achievement of Thornfield Maison, the final validation before the IPO that would turn his private empire into a public behemoth. Billions on the line. The fiancée at his side. The world watching. Everything he had ever wanted, crystallizing into permanence.

He touched the small of her back as he passed behind her chair. His hand was warm, his touch proprietary and gentle, and the gesture carried such a weight of practiced intimacy that for a vertiginous moment Jane almost believed it.
"How are you feeling?" he asked, settling into the chair across from her. His dark eyes held hers with an intensity that, in another life — a life where he was not what he was — might have been love.
"Nervous," she said, which was true enough.
"Good nervous or bad nervous?"
"Show nervous."
He smiled. The smile that had convinced boards of directors and seduced governments. "You'll be magnificent. You always are."
Jane ate her croissant and drank her perfect coffee and thought: *In four hours, this man's world ends. And I'm the one who's going to end it.*
She took another bite. It tasted like ash and butter.

The Carrousel du Louvre was a cathedral of commerce buried beneath the wings of the palace itself, and on the morning of a Fashion Week closing show, it was a construction site in holy robes. Jane slipped in through a service entrance at ten, flashing the all-access badge that Edward had given her weeks ago — another gesture of trust from a man who trusted no one, extended to the woman he believed was his.

The venue was cavernous. Work lights cast everything in a flat, industrial glare that stripped away the glamour and left only the machinery: rigging for the show lights, coiled cables thick as pythons, speakers the size of coffins, the raw steel bones of the runway that would be sheathed in white lacquer by afternoon. Technicians moved through the space with the purposeful exhaustion of people who had been doing this for a week and had one more show before they could sleep.

Jane walked the perimeter slowly, her flat shoes soundless on the concrete. She memorized sight lines — where the cameras would be positioned for the livestream, where the main screen would hang above the runway's terminus, where the audience seating rippled outward in concentric arcs. She noted the positions of the House of Thornfield's regular security team: two at the main entrance, two at the backstage access, one roaming. Standard. Manageable.

She found the main control booth on the mezzanine level, a glassed-in box overlooking the runway like a pilot's cabin. Inside, a technician was running cable checks. The booth would be Bertha's target — the server that fed both the in-venue screens and the livestream feed that would be broadcast to four continents. At 8:47 PM, Bertha's back door would override the input. The screens would switch. The evidence would play. Forty-seven seconds.
Jane nodded to herself and turned toward the backstage corridor.
And stopped.

There were six of them, stationed at intervals along the perimeter, and they were not Thornfield's usual people. Jane recognized Thornfield's security — she'd been studying them for weeks, cataloging their faces, their rotations, their habits. These were different. Private contractors, clearly, from the way they held themselves: the set of the shoulders, the placement of the hands, the way their eyes swept the room in overlapping fields of fire. Armed — she could see the print of holsters beneath their jackets, the hard outline of radio earpieces. Two more flanked the VIP access corridor. One stood near the control booth stairs.
Jane's stomach dropped.

Edward wasn't taking chances with the IPO. He was treating this like a state event — a fortress, not a fashion show. And why wouldn't he? Everything he'd built was converging on tonight. Of course he'd fortified it.

But fortified changed the calculus entirely. Jane forced herself to keep walking, face neutral, pace unhurried. She circled back through the backstage area, nodding to a dresser who recognized her, and calculated rapidly. If — *when* — the evidence triggered, Edward's first move would be to contain. He'd order the room cleared. The private security would move fast, professional, trained for exactly this kind of disruption. They could cut the livestream feed in under a minute if they reached the control booth. And once the screens went dark, there would be nothing. No evidence. No exposé. Just four hundred confused guests and Edward Rochester's voice smooth as teflon, explaining that there had been a technical malfunction, so sorry, the show will resume shortly.
She needed a backup.

Jane found a service corridor behind the dressing rooms — cold concrete underfoot, the smell of dust and industrial cleaner, the distant thrum of sound equipment being tested. She pressed her back against the wall, the concrete's chill seeping through her black turtleneck, and dialed Bertha.
It rang twice.
"Jane."

"Problem." She kept her voice low, barely above a whisper. "Extra security. Six private contractors. Not Thornfield's people — outside hires. Armed. If Edward orders the room cleared when we trigger, we lose the livestream window before the evidence reaches the feed. They'll cut it from the booth."
Silence on the line. Jane could hear Bertha breathing — slow, deliberate breaths, the sound of a mind running at full speed. Then:
"Then we don't just use the main screen." Bertha's voice was calm, but there was something new in it — a sharpness, a bright edge of intent. "We use everything."
"What do you mean, everything?"

"The livestream, yes. That's still the primary vector. But I'm going to air-drop the evidence to every device in the venue. Every phone, every tablet, every laptop within these walls. Personal hotspot protocol. They can't clear four hundred devices, Jane. They can't confiscate what's already in people's hands."
Jane pressed her palm flat against the cold concrete wall. "You can do that?"

"I built the Thornfield app, Jane." And now Bertha's voice carried something that might have been pride, or might have been fury — the two had always sounded the same in her mouth. "Every guest at this show downloaded it for the digital program. The seating chart, the designer notes, the exclusive behind-the-scenes content. That app is on four hundred phones. And I wrote the code. Every line of it. I can push to all of them simultaneously."

Jane closed her eyes. She could see it — four hundred screens lighting up at once, four hundred faces turning downward in unison, four hundred people watching the truth unfold in the palms of their hands. Edward could clear the room. He could cut the feed. He could burn the booth to the ground. And it wouldn't matter, because the evidence wouldn't be in one place. It would be everywhere.
"How long to deploy?" she asked.

"Same window. Forty-seven seconds. The back door triggers the main screen AND the app push simultaneously. Two vectors. Redundant delivery." A pause. "He can't stop it, Jane. Even if he figures out what's happening in real time — and he will, because he's not stupid — he can't put this back in the box. Once it's on their phones, it's on their cameras, their clouds, their group chats. It's out."

Jane opened her eyes. The service corridor was dim and cold and smelled like every back hallway in every venue in every city she'd ever worked in — concrete and dust and the ghost of a thousand shows that had come before. But the dread in her stomach had shifted, its weight redistributed into something that felt less like fear and more like purpose.
"Bertha."
"Yes?"
"Thank you."
A silence. Then, quietly: "Don't thank me yet. Thank me when it's done."
The line went dead.

Jane walked back through the venue. The transformation was already beginning — fabric draping over the steel bones, lights being angled and focused, the runway taking shape beneath the work lamps like a spine being assembled from nothing. It gleamed under the harsh light, a long white corridor stretching toward the place where the cameras would wait and the screens would hang and four hundred people would sit in judgment without knowing it.

In a few hours, models would walk this path. They would wear Thornfield's latest collection — the clean lines and dark elegance that had made the house a legend. And then Bertha would walk it, in the punk tuxedo with the hand-stitched lapels, carrying five years of evidence sewn into the seams like a soldier carries ammunition. And Jane would follow.

She would reach the end of the runway. She would unzip the oatmeal shell — that perfect, muted, forgettable gown — and step out of it, and underneath she would be crimson. Scarlet. The color of truth and violence and everything Edward Rochester had tried to bury.
Two women. Two garments. One truth.

Jane paused at the center of the runway. The work lights cast her shadow long and thin behind her, stretching toward the far end of the corridor as if her future self were already there, already waiting. The air smelled of fresh paint and electrical ozone. Somewhere behind her, a technician tested a speaker and a blast of orchestral music swelled and died. The concrete beneath her shoes was smooth and cold.
This was where it ended. Where it all ended.

She looked down the long, lit corridor and thought of four hundred phones, four hundred screens, four hundred witnesses who would carry the evidence out of this room and into the world. Edward could hire all the private security in Europe. He could lock every door, cut every wire, silence every speaker. He could not confiscate the world.
In a few hours, the truth would be everywhere — shimmering on every screen, gleaming like the runway under the work lights, bright and sharp and unstoppable as a blade.
Jane turned and walked back toward the light.
